transcript.transcript: Where were you were born? When I was born? 1890. 1890, and you were born in Finland? Finland. In Finland. Okay. Do you remember much of the boat trip over there? Oh, Not so much. No, I mean, how old were you when you came over? How old was I? Were you? Seven. You were seven? And your mother and father and you? No, just my mother and my brother. Just your mother and your brother? My father was at Inneville already that time. He was in Inneville? Uh-huh. In Idaho? And your family was coming to join him? This was in about 1897? This was in 1897 you came over here? Yeah, I came to the United States in 1897. Oh, in 1897. And you came directly to Idaho. Yeah. Okay. Now, what did your father do? What did your father do? Oh, he worked with his nephew. Making railroad ties and vent posts and all that kind of stuff. Oh, did he have his own business? Did he have his own business? No. He didn't. He worked for the mine? Well. Sort of. That didn't go in. Oh, it doesn't. They had the business of their own, you know, between the three men. Oh, OK. Now, can you tell me about Where you went to school when you were a child? And what the school was like? I went to school at Kingston, Idaho. Dudley, Idaho. At Latour Creek, Idaho. At Latour Creek. That's where my latest home. So was this grade school and high school? Was this grade school and high school? I never went to high school. They didn't think of high school those days very much. Oh, that's right. OK, now what was the school like? Was it one room? One room. It was just one room? Yeah. Did you have any- Oh, about 14 kids. 14? About 14, maybe 12 or 13, 14, you know. Was there any electricity? In the first school in the United States, I went to Kingston, Idaho School. One teacher, one room. How many grades? Eight grades. Oh, eight grades. All eight grades. Oh, wow. Now, how did you get to school? Wasn't there a story? Wasn't it very hard for you to get to school? What? How did you get to school? Didn't you have to cross a river? Oh yeah. Tell me the story of what every day going to school was like. Well, we walked. There was four of us kids and we walked about a mile along the railroad track. And then we had my dad and the other family who hired a fellow to take us across the river. Because we were all very small, you know, seven years old. And our folks hired a fellow to take us across the river, you know? Every day? At the school. It was right there on the bank. And you had to do this? So we didn't have to walk too much after we crossed the river. So you had to do this twice a day, going there and coming back? Twice a day you had to do this? Yes. Okay, now how did you like school? Oh fine, Very well. What was the discipline like? What was the discipline like? Oh, I don't know. I could just like any other school. Well, I mean, did the teacher have the ? Was it a woman or a man? Oh, he was a wonderful teacher, you know. No problems. No problems? Even if we couldn't talk any English. Oh, you spoke Finnish? Yeah. You spoke Finnish? Oh. Oh, yeah, sure. Oh. Was there a lot of Finnish people in this area? Oh, yes at that time they were There were a lot of people then. Okay. When did you get married? Nineteen... 1914 at Missoula, Montana. In Missoula, Montana. Missoula, Montana. Well, what were your... What were the social activities like? I mean like dances and was there many dances for the kids? Well, I don't know. It seems to me that it hasn't changed much. Well, were there a lot of festivals in the town of Kingston? Well, I don't recall. Oh, you don't recall. OK. Well, so When you and your husband got married, did you come back to Kingston from Missoula? No, we came back to Cataldo. Oh, you went to Cataldo. I was living in Cataldo with my mom. Oh, well, so what did your husband do then? Well, I would say he was all trade. He'd done anything he could. Any kind of work, you know, that he could get. Did he work for the mines? Yes, he did. He worked for Bunker Hill and some other mines. I don't remember what they are. But he worked 24 years at the zinc plant, and the last 24 years she worked at the zinc plant. Now what was the zinc plant like? What was it like working there? What were the working conditions like? Oh. Did your husband talk to you? I mean, was it really hot in there? Was there a lot of pollution? I remember Mrs. Johnson said that her husband got boils one time from working there. I can't tell about that in a way. I don't know much about it. Okay, well, if you want to, you can. You don't want to? Okay. Well, now, there was a mine, one time Mrs. Johnson said that there was a mine explosion. Oh, that's a mill. Could you tell me the story about it? At the mill? Uh-huh, Bunker Hill Mill. Oh, could you tell me the story of what happened? I don't know if I can tell. Oh, now, one time you told me. Well, I don't know no more except there was two explosions. Two explosions? At the... They claim at the mill. At the mill. And there was a bunch of us kids down at the railroad tracks, you know, there was a side track there. And it was Sunday morning and we were playing on the cars there, there was side tracks, you know, and there was two explosives that we heard very well. And we beat it home as hard as we could. Well, what year was this? I don't really know, but it was in 18... Must have been about, I don't know, maybe 1899 or I don't remember the year, you know, but I know we still lived at the end of those times. Was it, Did the miners set the explosion? I don't know. Well, what did everybody in the town say? Well, I imagine they claim it was the workers, you know. It was the workers. Why would they do this? Oh, well, as far as I can remember, I think they had some kind of working problems, you know, and maybe they were wanting more wages or something. I don't recall too much. Were they union at the time? Well, over there at Osborne, you know, they had a kind of fencing where they put all that they could find, all those men. Oh, they confined them all? Well, I guess most of them disappeared, but they find them, you know. Oh, they found them in and put them in? Oh, they find an awful lot of them. They sure treat them rough, too. Did they? They treat them pretty rough, huh? Because there was some people that we knew, you know, My mom and my dad knew some people, some of them you know. Was this the police who treated them rough? Yeah, so. Or was it the National Guard? Who was it? So I don't know anymore about it. It's so long ago. Was it the Army? Do you remember? I don't remember. No, but I remember last time we were talking, she said it was. Was it the National Guard? Did they call in the National Guard? I don't think they had the National Guard. They had the National Guard. They put them in a big pen? How many men? A couple hundred? Oh, probably even more. Oh, even more? I don't recall. Well, did they let them all go eventually? Yes, I guess in time. And maybe some of them might be even still in jail. Oh, wow. That's too bad. I imagine that he did already. Yeah, he probably would be. OK. Well, did you live near the river in Cataldo? Huh? Did you live near the river in Cataldo? Was there a river that went through Cataldo? Well, pretty close. Pretty close? Did it used to flood very much? Oh, yes, every year. Every year it used to flood? More or less. Sometimes very high, and sometimes not so high. Well, did it wreck any of the houses? Oh, sometimes. Sometimes it would? Every year? Well, the winter floods are worse than The spring floods. The winter floods? Yeah. Like you get some in December or January, you know. But they're the worst ones. From what? The snow's melting early? Well, there's more water, you know, that can do more damage. Yes, I guess so. Talk lounder. Oh, no, I think that's lounder. Can you hear me? Oh, me talk loud? Oh, me talk loud, Oh, okay. I was talking about the microphone. Okay. Now what was the population like? Was there a lot of... How many people were living in Cataldo at this time? Population? Oh, I don't know. About 50. About 50? It's just a farming country. What was it? I mean, was it a... In Cataldo, probably was, well, about maybe 50 people living around. Just 50 people? Because it's just a little farming town. Really small. Well, was there a mayor of the town? Or a? No. No mayor? I don't think so. Did you ever have town meetings? No. So the town didn't have any government at all? No, I don't know about that. Was there a sheriff of the town? No. No sheriff? Was there a? Well, there was. Sometimes there was. I don't know the last years, you know, when we were there, but... Well, what would happen when... Did anybody ever steal anything from their... In the town? Was there any crime in the town? No crime? No, I don't know. Was there any, was there, you mean, Did you have to keep your doors locked at night, or did, Well, if you wanted to. If you wanted to. But there wasn't... Because there were a lot of people that didn't. Oh, there was a lot of people that didn't. But, So everybody... In older times, you know, it wasn't as bad as it is now. You mean as far as locking your doors? Well, I don't remember if my folks kept doors locked or not, but I know after I had my own home, I did. Oh. In Cataldo? So what did you do? How many children did you have? How many children did you have? Five. Five children. Five boys. Five boys. And What are they doing today? Oh, I don't know if they're scattered all around. One of them works at filling the mines, or mill, and walls. One is in Othello, Washington. He works for the rescue squad, or whatever you. And two of them is in Vancouver, Washington. One works for Boise Cascades and the other works for all jobs he can find. And one of them is in California. He's retired. Oh. Well, did they like growing up in a small town? Did they like growing up in a small town? Oh, yeah. Did the town get very much bigger when you were raising your kids? Well, I don't know. Just that one large town. Well, what's it like today? Huh? What's the town like today, about the same? Well, what? I mean, is the town about the same size today? Same size? Maybe she can't hear me. There's a post office and a grocery store and a tavern, that's all it is. So what did you do for entertainment? Oh, we used to go to dances sometimes. In the town? In the hills and into the town, you know. Who put on the dances? Who put on the dances? Oh, gosh. Well, I mean, did you have a band there? Was there a band that played? Well, yes, and some of them, but not enough. We used to go down to the country dances, you know, at the private homes. Oh, at private homes? Yeah. What were those like? Yeah, so. Were they, what kind of music did they play? Oh, accordion. I don't remember. Accordion. Accordion? Was there anything else? I mean, was it just one guy with an accordion? Just one guy with an accordion. Wow. And everybody used to have good... Was it Finnish type of music? Yes. It was Finnish music. Of course. Almost all of them were Finns. Oh, almost all of them were Finns. Well, what other kind of music did they play? Country music? Well, I don't know what you really call it. But it was good dance? Waltzes and polkas mostly. Waltzes and polkas? Well, did the whole town, was this a kind of a big occasion? No. Was it? Just a neighborhood gathering and that's all. Oh just a neighborhood gathering? Oh. So What else did you do for entertainment? Oh, isn't that enough? Well, I mean... Those days you didn't need so much entertainment as you do nowadays. You had to work those days. Oh. So, well, how often would you have a dance? Why don't you come sit over here, Mrs. Jones? Why don't you come sit right here? Why do I have to sit there? Because that's the comfortable chair. I'll sit over here next to Mrs. Ratner. Sit there! You guys are all moving. You get over there. You get over there. Why don't you sit down? I'll sit down. Come on now, Mrs. Johnson. You're still doing it? Boy, the last time I was over here you guys had all sorts of stories, now you're clamming up on me. You're not asking nothing from me. Okay, yes I am. Okay, now Mrs. Johnson, we'll start with you. When were you born? Telco Idaho. Telco Idaho. And what year? 1901. 1901. Now you and Mrs. Ryder have sisters. I know that. Yes. And you were raised on a farm. Yes. Okay. With how many children? Along with how many children? Well, it was five of us. Five of you? And your father died when you were very young? Yes. Is that so? Yes, about five years old. So that left you and Mrs. Wright and brothers or sisters? One sister and one brother. So what were the chores like around the house? Well, everyday chores. Every day chores. Carrying wood, wash dishes, cook meals, milk cows, drive the horses. Drive the horses? Where did you drive them? Well, whatever was necessary to be done on the farm. Oh, you mean like plow and stuff? Yes, yes. That must have been some hard work. Well, naturally that was hard work. Have you ever been on a farm? Well, I've never been on a farm. That keeps you mighty busy. Keeps you mighty busy. Well, you didn't have any electricity? Oh, no. No electricity. And where did you get your water? From a well right close to the house. With a hand pump, we pumped it up, carried it to the house. Now, you told me that there was some pollution from the mine that was pretty bad when you were younger, was it bad? Right, yes. Now, which mine was this? Bunker Hill Mine, mostly. Bunker Hill Mine. Of course, there was a morning and the Hitchclone went off there, but Bunker Hill was the main one. Oh. Okay, now... And the water in that river looked just about like milk. Now, what year would this be? Like around 1910 or 1915? It was really bad then? Yes, it was bad right along until just recently, when the new law was coming. They can't put that stuff in the rivers anymore. They had to clear it up somehow. But the lands are all spoiled over there. They don't grow nothing anymore. Led it up. All led it up. Now, when you would bring your cattle near the river where the mine was dumping its waste, they wouldn't drink from it? No, I don't think they drank from the river. It was a small creek, close I guess. I don't really know where they drank. No, I mean, I thought one time you told me that the cows wouldn't drink from the river because they knew it was too polluted. Well, I don't think I ever said that. Oh, maybe I just got that wrong impression. Oh, okay. But it would kill some of the cows if they did drink from it, would it? Well, if they grazed on the land. Oh, if they grazed on the land. Oh, and usually put after the hay crop that fell out, or put them on a vintage grave in the fall, and several of them died. Just from that poison. And they would come from the high water that was on land, and they'd go down, and they'd leave a white scum on the ground. And it was pure arsenic. Pure arsenic? That's really dangerous. Oh sure it is. So just recently it got fixed? Yeah. Okay now, you told Donna one story about your husband working in his ink plant and what happened to him. I never did hear that story, so could you tell me that? What story? About how he got in the pollution. He got to. Oh, he was working in a cell room over there. And they had to wear rubber clothes over there. And he had rubber gloves on. And it started itching in between his fingers. And it just spread all over his whole system, and it just boils, just like boils, so thick that he was in the hospital for eight days. And they couldn't hardly give him a shot, they couldn't find a spot on his body, even his tongue was all covered in tears. God, that must have been terrible. Sure, he was really in misery. I bet. You must have been scared. And then the doctor said that definitely does not come from the zinc blood. But he said it did. The doctor said it did not come from the zinc? Yes. Was he a company doctor? Well, he wasn't... I don't know what he... He wasn't a Spokane skin specialist and come from there, but he had been a Kellogg doctor before. Oh. So imagine, had to protect the company. Imagine that? Gee... So it finally went away. Yeah, it finally cleared up. Did he quit the zinc plant? No, no, he worked there for 23 years until he retired. Now, did this happen to a lot of men who worked there in his inkblot? Well, there is some that did get it. But I suppose more are allergic than... Others are more allergic than the others too. Yeah, that's probably... And a lot of them had trouble getting ulcers and things like that. Those fields were terrible, and places, and at times. Boy, way back then, in 1915, too. That's just amazing. Well, that zinc plant didn't start running before, I think in 19... No, that was around 1928 when the zinc plants started running. Oh, 1928. Okay, now, could you tell me what it was like when you were going to school? What the... Did you have to walk very far? Well, we walked about half a mile or so to school. That's not too bad. There was around 30 kids in school, most of them were thin kids. All of them were thin kids? Most all, three or four of them. Oh. All eight grades and one teacher. All in the same room, naturally. All in the same room. So you can imagine, the teacher had a lot of fun those days. It paid about $35 a month. So would she teach a few of you, Would she teach a couple of grades at a time? Would she say, at first grade, I want you to work on this while I teach the seventh or eighth grade? Oh, well, she just assigned us lessons. She just would have assigned lessons. Well, I see. And the only thing, the sixth grade, there was usually less in the sixth grade, so she just tried to push them to go with the others. Well, I see. So once you got out of the eighth grade, that was about most of the schooling people had back then, wasn't it? Yeah, that was all the schooling we knew about those days. Because there was too much work to be done when you got older on the farm? Yes, and another thing, there wasn't enough money those days. You know, it takes money. You would have had to go to high school and Kellogg and board up and things like that when you don't have hardly any income. You know, you don't go places. Well, how much was the average? What was your family living on as far as money went back then? Do you remember? I don't know. We tried to milk cows and ship cream to the creamery and maybe get $3 or $4 for a five-gallon can of cream. And we tried to raise potatoes. That's about the only thing that grew on that ground. And just about tried to live on that. Wow. You know, that was a little rosy those days. Really? But you thought, you didn't really know, did you realize how hard it was then? No. I mean, It seemed like everybody was doing that? Everybody was doing it. We didn't know nothing else. That was just it. So when you were a kid, what was really fun for you to do? Can you remember what you really liked to do when you were a kid? I don't know what I really like to do, and I don't know what would have really been fun. You know, like kids today, they watch Bug Bunny on the TV when they're younger. Yeah, but those days they had no TVs or radios or nothing. Wow, so... Nothing about them. So there wasn't... Did you like to read? Was that kind of a pastime? No, I'd never been a bookworm. Never had been much? No. Well, now, when you needed to take a bath, what did the family do? Oh, we had a sauna. You had a sauna? Yeah, and that's where you really could get yourself clean. Way back then they had saunas, huh? Well, families at the time, they really had them. The young people really had them. So they came over from Finland, huh? Yes. So you could go take a sauna anytime you want or the whole family take one at one time? The whole family took it at one time because you had to heat the bathhouse up. It wasn't like it is nowadays, just turn the faucet. So five or six of you would take a... Well, once you heat it, you could take several, you know, take turns. It might take time to heat it. A big pile of rocks, and then a fireplace inside of that, and get the rocks all red hot. Not red hot, but real hot. And then once you were all nice and warm, I bet you hated to go back outside, huh? Well, then you really felt nice and clean. Really perspired, so all the pores were really clean. That's amazing that you had that back then. Sure, that was the best thing of all. So you graduated from school, you were about, what, 13 or 14? About 13. About 13. So then it was working on the farm full time? Yes. Yes. And then until how old were you when you left? Well, what did you do now in your dating? What were the dating customs like back then? Did you just date boys that you met in school, or did your brothers try to fix you up with dates? Well, just whoever happened to come along. Whoever happened to ask you. Well, I mean, what was the courting like? Wasn't there customs about, did you have to have chaperones when you went? No, nothing like a chaperone even less those days. You didn't have chaperones? I always heard that that's the way it was. So where did you used to go when a boy would come over? Did you just hang around the house? Did you go over to his place? I don't know. Just sat outside. On the front porch? I don't know. Oh yeah, they'd build a dance hall right close to our home. We used to have a dance over there quite often. So a lot of times in the evening when your boyfriend would come over, you'd just, a lot of times, just sit on the porch and sit on the swing? Yes, yes. Did you have problems with your brothers and sisters always coming around? No. Trying to see what you were doing? No. Oh they didn't? They just left you alone? As far as I remember. That's too long ago. That's too long ago. I don't think so. I think you remember that. I remember. I remember my mother telling me that. So then you got married in when? 1920. 1920. And you and your husband, where did you go? You were living in Cataldo at the time. Yes. You stayed? Yes. I bought a place over there, and that's where we moved to, in Cataldo. Was it a little farm? Well, I guess you might call it a little stump farm. Little stump farm. Little farm with lots of work. Lots of work. Digging ground and... Do you remember, so this was in 1920, right? Do you remember the first time like you saw an airplane flying? I don't remember. You don't remember? I don't remember nothing about the airplane. So I never actually happened to even see one. Seems like it would be pretty exciting the first time. You'd see one. I don't know. Wasn't that exciting? I don't remember any way that would have been so exciting. Well, now, as far as transportation, did you all have cars, or did you use horses much? Well, after we got married, none of them bought a car. But before, those days, you used your feet to get places. I'd say, no, I'll have to go to the grocery store to get dry the horses. Oh. What about doctors, if you had to go to the doctor? I either had to go up to Kellogg to a doctor, or what else do you want? Ride the train to get to the doctor. Oh, ride the train. Well, now when you and your husband got married, and then he worked as a carpenter? He did carpenter work, and he worked at some logging camps until he went to the zinc plant and worked there. Weren't the logging camps, I heard they were pretty rough. Well, I guess they were rough. You can imagine a fellow walking, trapping out in the woods all day long, sawing logs, and if he had to haul them down, that would work. Did they allow liquor in the logging camp?

lines.line: # Economic Struggles and Substinance Farming
> Economic hardships faced by farming families in the early 20th century are recalled, including the struggle to make a living from agriculture.
** economic hardships, farming, agriculture, 20th century, livelihood **

children.text: # Family Background and Early Life

> The conversation begins with the interviewee discussing their birth year (1890) and birthplace (Finland), their family's emigration to the United States at age seven, and the relocation to Idaho. The interviewee mentions their father's work situation upon their arrival.
** Finland, 1890, emigration, Idaho, family, father's work **
# Father's Occupation and Family Settlement

> Details about the father's occupation in Idaho are discussed. He worked with his nephew making railroad ties and vent posts but did not own a business. The family had a business arrangement with three men.
** occupation, railroad ties, business, family, Idaho **
# Education and School Life
> The interviewee recalls their schooling in Idaho, attending grade school at different locations, primarily in a one-room schoolhouse. High school attendance was uncommon during those days.
** education, school, Idaho, one-room schoolhouse, grade school **
# School Transportation and Daily Routine
> A story unfolds about how the interviewee and other children had to walk along a railroad track and cross a river to get to school using a hired person's assistance.
** school transportation, river crossing, daily routine, children **
# Marriage and Social Life
> The interviewee talks about marriage in Missoula, Montana (1914), and discusses social activities such as dances and gatherings in the town. The family later settled in Cataldo, Idaho.
** marriage, Missoula, social life, dances, Cataldo **
# Husband's Occupation and Work Conditions
> The husband's work at various mines, including 24 years at a zinc plant, is discussed. Conditions at the zinc plant, including pollution and health impacts, are highlighted.
** husband's work, mines, zinc plant, work conditions, pollution **
# Mill Explosions and Labor Disputes
> The interviewee recalls explosions at a mill and the associated labor disputes and tensions, which resulted in harsh treatment of workers and involvement from authorities.
** mill explosions, labor disputes, workers, authorities **
# Living by the River and Environmental Challenges
> Experiences living close to a river that frequently flooded, causing damage to homes and impacting the community, are recounted. The interviewee also touches on the small population of Cataldo.
** river, flooding, housing damage, Cataldo, community **
# Community Life and Family
> The interviewee talks about the lack of formal governance in the town and recalls family life, including raising children and their subsequent careers.
** community, governance, family, children, careers **
# Entertainment and Musical Gatherings
> Discussion about entertainment options available, with emphasis on dances at private homes, accordion music, and Finnish cultural influences.
** entertainment, dances, music, Finnish culture **
# Reminiscing About Youth and Daily Life
> Memories of growing up are shared, including schooling, daily chores, and simple pleasures in a time without modern conveniences.
** youth, chores, daily life, schooling, no electricity **
# Impacts of Mining Pollution
> Details about the pollution from mining activities affecting local agriculture and cattle health are discussed, along with efforts to mitigate these issues.
** mining, pollution, agriculture, cattle health, mitigation **
# Husband's Health Issues from Work
> The interviewee recounts a significant health scare their husband faced due to working in harsh conditions at a zinc plant, emphasizing occupational hazards.
** husband, health issues, work conditions, zinc plant, occupational hazards **
# Economic Struggles and Substinance Farming
> Economic hardships faced by farming families in the early 20th century are recalled, including the struggle to make a living from agriculture.
** economic hardships, farming, agriculture, 20th century, livelihood **
# Cultural Heritage and Daily Customs
> The family's Finnish heritage is reflected in their customs, like using a sauna, which played a significant role in their weekly routine.
** Finnish heritage, sauna, customs, daily routine **

story.title: Johnson_Mayme_OH 1653_A
story.description: Sisters Mayme Johnson (1901-1996) and Hilda Wright (1890-1982) are interviewed by Brian Markey on their lives. Interviewed March 3, 1976.
